articles.title: Trace Timer B and downstream progress when call setup times out
articles.summary: Call setup timeout incidents usually need timer-aware tracing rather than generic restarts.
content: When to use: INVITE requests are accepted but the session stalls until the caller hits a setup timeout.
Action: Trace the call against Timer B or the equivalent setup watchdog and identify the first hop that stops returning progress.
Why it works: Timeout incidents are defined by where progress stops, so timer-aware tracing narrows the broken stage quickly.
Verify: After remediation, provisional responses and final answers arrive before the watchdog expires.
Evidence: Capture the exact hop and timer value that caused the timeout for future retrieval.

articles.title: Verify session refresh handling for mid-call stability issues
articles.summary: Mid-session drops often come from failed refresh or re-INVITE handling rather than transport failure alone.
content: When to use: Calls establish successfully but drop later around a refresh interval or re-INVITE event.
Action: Check session timer negotiation, refresh ownership, and whether the refresh message actually completes on time.
Why it works: Broken refresh handling can terminate otherwise healthy sessions at predictable intervals.
Verify: Calls survive beyond the previous drop window and refresh transactions complete consistently.
Guardrail: Keep both endpoints aligned on supported session timer behavior before changing global defaults.

articles.title: Inspect destination capacity before treating busy responses as user behavior
articles.summary: A busy-destination spike can indicate overloaded application resources rather than normal callee unavailability.
content: When to use: Busy or congestion responses spike across many calls or one destination group at the same time.
Action: Check downstream application capacity, queue depth, and any per-destination admission controls before accepting the busy signal at face value.
Why it works: Platform congestion can create artificial busy responses that need infrastructure remediation, not user retrial advice.
Verify: Busy ratios return to normal as capacity or admission pressure is relieved.
Documentation: Preserve the affected destination group or service cluster in the incident notes.

articles.title: Stabilize media-anchor allocation for calls that fail after setup
articles.summary: If media resources are exhausted or slow to allocate, sessions may establish and then fail quickly.
content: When to use: SIP signaling reaches the expected stage, but media anchoring or allocation lags cause late failure.
Action: Review media-anchor capacity, allocation latency, and any shared port or relay pool exhaustion.
Why it works: Session stability depends on both signaling and media readiness, especially in anchored deployments.
Verify: Calls complete setup with media in place and no longer drop during the early active phase.
Risk: Moving media anchors under load can affect active calls, so prefer incremental capacity relief.

articles.title: Tune NAT keepalive intervals when established sessions disappear unexpectedly
articles.summary: Aggressive NAT expiry can drop otherwise healthy mid-session traffic.
content: When to use: Calls drop after a period of silence or reduced traffic and the path crosses NAT or firewall state.
Action: Compare keepalive cadence to the actual state timeout on the path and adjust it so bindings remain fresh.
Why it works: The session may be healthy at the SIP layer while network state silently expires underneath it.
Verify: Calls remain stable through the prior idle window and no longer show the same post-idle drop signature.
Operator hint: Keep the NAT timeout evidence with the incident so network and session teams share the same context.

articles.title: Compare early-media negotiation on successful and failing calls
articles.summary: Early-media mismatches can delay answer handling or create one-way session failures.
content: When to use: Some calls stall during ringing or fail immediately after early media is offered.
Action: Compare SDP and signaling around provisional responses to see whether early media expectations diverge between endpoints.
Why it works: Early-media defects are subtle and often look like generic timeout or drop events in high-level metrics.
Verify: Calls progress normally through provisional media phases and answer timing returns to normal.
Follow-up: Preserve one passing and one failing early-media trace for retrieval quality.

articles.title: Review admission-control thresholds before scaling everything
articles.summary: Overly strict session thresholds can create artificial busy or timeout behavior.
content: When to use: Busy or timeout symptoms appear at load levels that should be sustainable for the platform.
Action: Inspect session admission limits, concurrency caps, and protection thresholds on the path handling the calls.
Why it works: Conservative guardrails may trip long before the infrastructure is truly saturated.
Verify: Adjusted thresholds allow healthy calls through without creating new overload elsewhere.
Risk: Raise limits carefully and pair the change with close monitoring of downstream service health.

articles.title: Capture BYE or teardown loss when calls end unexpectedly
articles.summary: Unexpected call drops often correlate with missing BYE handling or broken teardown signaling.
content: When to use: Calls terminate abruptly and logs suggest a missing or late teardown sequence.
Action: Trace BYE, CANCEL, and error paths to see whether the session is closing cleanly or being abandoned after network loss.
Why it works: Understanding teardown behavior separates genuine drops from controlled session release.
Verify: The teardown path becomes complete and unexpected drop counts fall.
Evidence: Keep one full teardown sequence attached to the incident for reuse.

articles.title: Validate codec offer-answer compatibility on unstable session paths
articles.summary: Codec mismatch can surface as timeout, early failure, or mid-call instability.
content: When to use: Calls fail around answer processing or drop shortly after setup on specific endpoint pairings.
Action: Compare offered and accepted codecs, payload types, and media directions between successful and failing calls.
Why it works: Some session incidents are really interoperability problems hidden behind generic SIP symptoms.
Verify: The corrected codec policy eliminates the failure on the affected endpoint pairing.
Guardrail: Avoid broad codec removals without checking wider subscriber compatibility.

articles.title: Shift load away from a degraded session-processing pool
articles.summary: Session incidents can localize to one overloaded pool even when aggregate health looks acceptable.
content: When to use: Failures align with one service pool, node group, or region while peers remain mostly healthy.
Action: Drain or deprioritize the degraded pool and steer new sessions to healthier capacity.
Why it works: Localized session pressure creates uneven user impact and can be mitigated faster than a full-stack change.
Verify: Failure volume follows the pool out of service and healthy pools absorb the traffic without new saturation.
Risk: Confirm the destination pools have enough headroom before shifting live session load.
